agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 Annual Municipal Report presented to Council
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to receive and consider the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and a shorter Highlights version. This report summarizes the city's finances, services, and achievements for the past year. It is a proposed overview for council's information and discussion, not a final decision or new law. No immediate changes to business rules or fees are proposed in this item.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
2025 ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT AND ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT HIGHLIGHTS
Purpose: To present the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and 2025 Annual Municipal Report Highlights.

agenda_briefs.title: Burnaby to replace Development Procedures Bylaw, amend Building and Fees Bylaws — effective July 1, 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on repealing the current Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw (2022) and replacing it with a new Development Procedures Bylaw (2026), effective July 1, 2026. The proposal also includes amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw (2016) and the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. If approved, these changes would update rules and fees for development applications, building permits, and related processes, including new Development Permit types and formalized fee and refund practices.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on May 12, 2026 — if approved, new application rules and fees take effect July 1, 2026, affecting your development or building project.

key_dates.label: Council vote on new Development Procedures Bylaw — Burnaby
New Development Procedures Bylaw and amendments take effect — Burnaby

agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
PROPOSED REPEAL AND REPLACEMENT OF BURNABY DEVELOPMENT PROCEDURES BYLAW AND RELATED AMENDMENTS TO THE BURNABY BUILDING BYLAW AND BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW
Purpose:
To seek Council approval to repeal
Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw, 2022
and replace it with the proposed
Development Procedures Bylaw, 2026
, to amend the
Burnaby
Building Bylaw, 2016
and
Burnaby
Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw.

attachments.title: Proposed Repeal and Replacement of Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw and Rela

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to apply for UBCM award on development approvals
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider endorsing an application for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards. This is based on the final report for Phase 3 of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program. The DAP program aims to speed up and simplify how the city approves development projects. If endorsed, the application would recognize Burnaby's work on making the approval process faster and more efficient for builders and developers.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you right now. Watch for the council decision on this endorsement.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
DAP PROGRAM - PHASE 3 FINAL REPORT
Purpose: To provide an update on the activities and outcomes of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program and seek endorsement to apply for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for mobile devices and data plans
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services. If approved, this contract would set the terms for how the city purchases and manages mobile technology for its operations. The decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
CA - MOBILE DEVICE AND PLANS
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on aeration and overseeding contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for aeration and overseeding services. If approved, this would mean the city hires a company to aerate (poke small holes in the soil to let air, water, and nutrients reach the roots) and overseed (spread new grass seed over existing lawns) on city-owned land. This is a routine maintenance contract and does not create new rules or fees for local businesses. The decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
CA - AERATION AND OVERSEEDING SERVICES
Purpose:
T
o obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for aeration and overseeding services.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for Victory Street upgrades from Patterson to Jubilee
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for the Victory Street upgrades project between Patterson Avenue and Jubilee Road. If approved, this would fund construction work to improve the road, sidewalks, and utilities in that area. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider the contract award at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
CA - (CB-3233) VICTORY ST. - PATTERSON TO JUBILEE ROAD UPGRADES
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Victory St. – Patterson to Jubilee Road Upgrades project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on sidewalk contract for five Burnaby streets
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for new sidewalks on Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison streets. If approved, the project would add or replace sidewalks in these areas. This is a proposed decision — it has not been finalized yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.7
CA - (CA-3299) TRIUMPH, CARLETON, ROSSER, GILMORE, AND MADISON SIDEWALK PACKAGE
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison Sidewalk Package project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Contract change for Burnaby's Sidewalk Expansion Program
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change (a formal amendment to an existing agreement) for the Sidewalk Expansion Program (project EB-8022). If approved, this change would alter the terms of the city's contract to build or expand sidewalks. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CC - (EB-8022) SIDEWALK EXPANSION PROGRAM
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) to the (EB-8022) Sidewalk Expansion Program.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider comment letters on Metro 2050 land-use amendments for Maple Ridge and Surrey
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider sending comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District about two proposed changes to the regional growth strategy, Metro 2050. One change is from the City of Maple Ridge (for land near North 256 Street) and the other is from the City of Surrey (for the Hazelmere area). These amendments would allow different types of development in those areas. Council has not yet decided what to say in the letters — this is a chance for the city to give its feedback before the regional district makes a final decision.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.9
COMMENT LETTERS ON PROPOSED METRO 2050 AMENDMENTS: CITY OF MAPLE RIDGE (NORTH 256 STREET) and SURREY (HAZELMERE)
Purpose:
To inform Council about two different site-specific Metro 2050 Amendments, one proposed by the City of Maple Ridge and the other by the City of Surrey, and to consider forwarding comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to review street width standards
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a motion that would direct city staff to review Burnaby's street width standards. This means the city would look at how wide streets need to be for new developments. If approved, this review could lead to changes in the rules for how wide streets must be built in the future. The goal is to see if current standards are still appropriate or if they should be updated. This is a first step — no new rules have been passed yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.1
COTW - PROPOSED WORKS AND SERVICES REQUIREMENTS
Purpose: To direct staff to review the City's street width standards.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 2026 consultant budget cuts list
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a staff request to prepare a detailed list of consultant budget cuts for 2026. If approved, staff will identify which consultant contracts to cut, which departments are affected, and which consultants would keep their funding. This is a proposed step — no cuts have been made yet. The decision will shape how the city spends money on outside experts next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, it could affect businesses that work as city consultants.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.2
FMC - 2026 CONSULTANT FUNDNG AND BUDGET REDUCTIONS
Purpose: To seek Council approval for staff to provide a comprehensive list of consultant budget cuts, including associated functions and departments, and a separate list of consultants retaining funding for 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan update for council direction
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider giving direction on the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan. This proposed plan would set new rules for how trails on Burnaby Mountain are used, maintained, and managed. It could affect businesses that offer guided hikes, mountain biking tours, or other outdoor recreation services on the mountain. The plan is not yet approved — council is being asked for feedback and direction at this stage.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, new trail rules could affect your outdoor recreation business on Burnaby Mountain.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.3
PRCC - DRAFT BURNABY MOUNTAIN TRAIL MANAGEMENT PLAN
Purpose: To provide an update and seek Council direction on the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider supporting Vancouver Roques Rugby's Bingham Cup 2028 bid
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a request from Vancouver Roques Rugby to support their bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028. The Bingham Cup is an international rugby tournament for gay and inclusive teams. Council's support would mean the City backs the bid, which could bring visitors and economic activity to the region. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.4
PRCC - VANCOUVER ROQUES RUGBY - BINGHAM CUP 2028 SUPPORT REQUEST
Purpose: To seek Council support for Vancouver Roques Rugby's bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue. Non-market housing means rental homes that are priced below the usual market rate, often run by a non-profit or government agency. This project is still in the proposal stage and has not been approved yet. Council will consider the update and decide on next steps at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this project could affect nearby property values and development plans in the area.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
PROPOSED NON-MARKET HOUSING PROJECT AT 3961 AND 3983 NORLAND AVENUE
Purpose: To provide an update on the proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 to replace 1965 version
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a major update to the city's zoning rules. A proposed bylaw, called Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, would repeal the current 1965 Zoning Bylaw and replace it with a completely new set of rules. This includes new Development Permit Area Guidelines (rules for getting permits to build or change a property) and a reassignment of zoning districts (which decides what you can do on a piece of land, like build a house, a store, or a factory). This is a first, second, and third reading of the bylaw, meaning it is still being debated and has not been approved yet. If passed, it would change what businesses can do with their properties and how new developments are approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit written comments to Burnaby City Council before the vote to share how this bylaw would affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 public hearing date

agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14815 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to repeal Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, including new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update zoning penalty rules for new 2026 bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. The change would replace old references to the 1965 Zoning Bylaw with the new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. This means that when the new zoning rules take effect, the city will be able to issue tickets and fines for breaking those rules. For business owners, this is a housekeeping update — it makes sure the enforcement system matches the latest zoning rules. The proposal is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed now — just be aware that once approved, zoning fines will be enforced under the new 2026 bylaw.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2
#14816 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 4, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (repeal Schedule A, Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 section and replace with Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to amend definitions for affordable housing fee waivers
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the definitions of 'affordable housing' and 'Zoning Bylaw' in the current Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges (ACC) and Development Cost Charges (DCC) Bylaw. Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges are fees the city charges developers to help pay for community needs like parks, daycare, and roads that come with new development. The proposed change would update what counts as 'affordable housing' under this bylaw, which could affect which housing projects qualify for fee reductions or waivers. This is an amendment to the 2024 bylaw and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect eligibility for fee waivers on affordable housing projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.3
#14817 - Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges Bylaw 2024, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of ACC and DCC Bylaw 2024 (definitions of "affordable housing" and "Zoning Bylaw")

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 for redevelopment impacts
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026. This bylaw would create new rules to protect tenants who are forced to move because their building is being redeveloped (torn down or rebuilt). The goal is to give tenants more rights and support during the redevelopment process. Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to tell Council your views on the proposed bylaw.
agenda_briefs.industries: Tenant, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.4
#14821 - Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish protections for tenants impacted by redevelopment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to strengthen tenant protection enforcement
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. This change would update how the city enforces rules that protect tenants. If approved, it could mean new or stronger penalties for landlords who break tenant protection rules. The goal is to make it easier for the city to hold landlords accountable and give tenants better protection under local law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect how tenant complaints are handled in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.5
#14822 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (tenant protection)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to vote on mid-year fee and charge updates for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the City of Burnaby's Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw with mid-year changes for 2026. If approved, this would adjust various fees the city charges for permits, licences, services, and other municipal costs. The exact fee changes are not listed in this agenda item, but business owners should watch for the council decision to see if their specific licence or permit fees will go up or down.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect your business licence or permit fees.
agenda_briefs.industries: Liquor, Cannabis, Construction & Development, Food Service, Hotel & Accommodation, Spa & Wellness, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Grocery & Food Retail, Retail, Healthcare, Finance & Insurance, Technology, Natural Resources, Transportation & Logistics, Agriculture, Labour & Employment, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.6
#14823 - Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (2026 mid-year amendments)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and e-scooter pilot bylaw amendment up for final vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of an amendment to the Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw that would create a pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, the bylaw would allow these devices on certain streets and paths under new rules. This is a proposed change — it has not been passed yet. The vote will happen at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, the new rules could affect how you operate delivery services or use e-bikes for your business.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1
#14811 - Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on final adoption of e-bike pilot program in parks
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw amendment that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in certain city parks as part of a pilot program. The proposed change to the Parks Regulation Bylaw would create a temporary trial period to test how these electric devices can be used safely in parks. If approved, the pilot program would set specific rules about where and how e-bikes and e-scooters can be ridden. This is not yet law — Council will make the final decision at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, the pilot program rules will affect where you can ride e-bikes and e-scooters in Burnaby parks.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.2
#14812 - Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment for e-bike and e-scooter pilot program
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 to support a new pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, this change would update how the city enforces rules for these devices during the pilot. The amendment is part of a broader effort to test and regulate electric mobility options in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect how e-bike and e-scooter rules are enforced in your business area.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.3
#14813 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 sets new property tax rates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would set the tax rates for all taxable land and buildings (called 'improvements') in the city for the 2026 tax year. It is based on sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter, which give the city the authority to set these rates. If approved, this bylaw will determine how much property tax you will owe next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will directly affect your property tax bill for 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 final adoption vote

agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.4
#14814 - Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish tax rates upon all taxable land and improvements pursuant to sections 197 and 216 of the
Community Charter

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to abandon rezoning for 3900 Grand Promenade, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on abandoning a proposed rezoning bylaw for properties at 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858/9898 Gatineau Place. The original plan, approved in September 2024, allowed two residential towers (80 and 73 storeys) with a 13-storey retail and office base. The applicant now wants to remove the office space and add 292 more market strata residential units instead. Council will consider whether to scrap the old rezoning and let the applicant start fresh with a new proposal.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if abandoned, a new rezoning application will be filed, which may affect nearby property values and development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.3.1
#14737 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2025 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858 and 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 and 73 storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. The applicant is proposing to remove commercial office use and replace it with additional market strata residential use, which includes the proposal of 292 additional market strata units
